message.content:
You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (a dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends import-guarded; transcripts committed + scrubbed intentionally). It has survived EIGHT hardening passes and CI is currently green — easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode and a file:line/symbol location; no style nits. If a dimension is clean, return few/zero findings — do NOT invent. Read real code (Read/Grep/Bash; "python -m pytest", "source .venv/bin/activate"). The branch is harden/pass-9 (just branched from merged main @ pass 8).

YOUR DIMENSION: security/data-sovereignty.
Local-URI guards across ALL backends, injection, unsafe deserialization (pickle/yaml), path traversal, SSRF, secret handling. Now that K-SEC-1 opened Unix sockets, re-audit _local.py for a bypass.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll audit the security/data-sovereignty dimension of the hc-assay engine, focusing on local-URI guards, the new Unix socket support (K-SEC-1), and deserialization/injection/SSRF vectors.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && find . -name "_local.py" -not -path "*/.venv/*" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; grep -rln "socket\|AF_UNIX\|local_uri\|file://\|localhost\|127.0.0.1" --include="*.py" . | grep -v ".venv" | head -40

---
tests/test_reasoning.py
tests/test_persistence.py
tests/test_observability.py
tests/integration/conftest.py
tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py
src/assay_engine/_local.py
src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py
src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py
src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py
src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py

1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def is_local_socket_path(host: str | None) -> bool:
42	    """True for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host — a leading-'/' absolute directory (#K-SEC-1).
43	
44	    libpq accepts ``host=/var/run/postgresql`` (or the query form ``?host=/tmp``) to connect over
45	    a local Unix-domain socket — a connection that NEVER touches the network, so it is strictly
46	    more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. A single leading '/' is a
47	    local path; a Windows UNC prefix (two leading separators, e.g. ``//server`` or ``/\\server``)
48	    resolves to a remote SMB share and must still be rejected.
49	    """
50	    if not host:
51	        return False
52	    h = host.strip()
53	    return h.startswith("/") and not h.startswith("//") and not h.startswith("/\\")
54	
55	
56	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
57	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
58	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
59	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
60	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
61	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
62	            f"from {url!r}"
63	        )
64	    return url
65	
66	
67	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
68	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
69	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
70	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
71	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
72	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
73	        )
74	    return host
75	
76	
77	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
78	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
79	
80	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
81	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
82	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
83	
84	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
85	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
86	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
87	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
88	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
89	    """
90	    if "@" in netloc:
91	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
92	    hosts: list[str] = []
93	    for part in netloc.split(","):
94	        p = part.strip()
95	        if not p:
96	            continue
97	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
98	            end = p.find("]")
99	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
100	        else:
101	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
102	    return hosts
103	
104	
105	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
107	
108	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
109	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
110	    reports, since the two can disagree:
111	
112	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
113	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
114	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
115	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
116	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
117	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
118	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
119	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
120	
121	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
122	    """
123	    try:
124	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
125	        netloc = parsed.netloc
126	        query = parsed.query
127	    except ValueError:
128	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
129	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
130	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
131	        ) from None
132	
133	    if netloc:
134	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
135	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
136	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
137	        if "\\" in netloc:
138	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
139	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
140	            )
141	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
142	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
143	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
144	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
145	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
146	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
147	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
148	                )
149	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
150	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
151	                h = val.strip()
152	                # A Unix-domain-socket directory (leading '/') is local and accepted (#K-SEC-1).
153	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
154	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
155	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
156	                        f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
157	                    )
158	        return uri
159	
160	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
161	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
162	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
163	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
164	                # A Unix-domain-socket directory (leading '/') is local and accepted (#K-SEC-1).
165	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
166	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
167	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
168	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
169	                    )
170	        return uri
171	
172	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
173	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
174	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
175	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
176	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
177	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
178	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
179	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
180	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
181	        )
182	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
183

content.text: Now let me look at the new K-SEC-1 socket path logic closely. There's a potential bypass: `is_local_socket_path` accepts a single leading `/`, but libpq's `host` parameter with a socket directory plus a multi-host form. Let me check how these guards interact with the actual postgres host parameter, and look for a bypass in the multi-host DSN / socket abstract namespace.

content.text: Now let me examine the key bypass concern. The `host` query parameter in libpq, when given a socket dir, libpq actually requires it differently. But more importantly — let me check the multi-host authority interaction with the socket-path acceptance, and the checkpoint usage which is the postgres path.

content.content:
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``.
141	
142	    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
143	    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
144	    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
145	    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
146	    ``://[REDACTED]@`` userinfo and ``password=[REDACTED] tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
147	    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
148	    "always precise".
149	    """
150	    if conn_str:
151	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
152	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
153	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
154	
155	
156	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
157	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
158	
159	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
160	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
161	    """
162	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
163	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
164	
165	
166	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
167	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
168	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
169	    that can raise)."""
170	    try:
171	        pool.close()
172	    except Exception:
173	        pass
174	
175	
176	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
177	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
178	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
179	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
180	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
183	    for p in pools:
184	        _safe_close(p)
185	
186	
187	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
188	    global _atexit_registered
189	    with _init_lock:

content.text: Now let me test the actual bypass hypotheses against the code. Let me focus on the K-SEC-1 socket path. Key question: does libpq treat the `host` URI query parameter as overriding, and is there a multi-host bypass where one host is a socket path and another is remote? Also: the authority-host check rejects non-loopback, but the socket-path acceptance is ONLY in the query-param branch — that's fine. Let me check a real bypass: percent-encoding in the query, and the abstract namespace, and most critically the **authority + query interaction where authority host is loopback but `hostaddr` is remote** — that's handled. Let me run actual tests.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python3 -c "
from src.assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError, is_local_socket_path, is_loopback_host

def chk(uri):
    try:
        require_local_uri(uri, what='t')
        return 'ACCEPT'
    except NonLocalEndpointError:
        return 'REJECT'

# Candidate bypasses around K-SEC-1 socket-path acceptance
tests = [
    # libpq: host param percent-encoded in query
    'postgresql://localhost/db?host=%2Fvar%2Frun%2Fpostgresql',  # encoded /var/run -> socket, local
    'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com',  # multi-value? actually one value
    'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp&host=evil.com',  # two host values, 2nd remote
    'postgresql:///db?host=/tmp',  # no authority, query socket
    # DSN: host with socket + a second remote via comma (libpq multi-host)
    \"host='/tmp,evil.com' port=5432 dbname=x\",
    'host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x',  # libpq splits comma -> /tmp and evil.com
    'host=/tmp,/var dbname=x',
    'hostaddr=1.2.3.4 host=/tmp dbname=x',
]
for t in tests:
    print(chk(t), repr(t))
"

content.content:
ACCEPT 'postgresql://localhost/db?host=%2Fvar%2Frun%2Fpostgresql'
ACCEPT 'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com'
REJECT 'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp&host=evil.com'
ACCEPT 'postgresql:///db?host=/tmp'
ACCEPT "host='/tmp,evil.com' port=5432 dbname=x"
ACCEPT 'host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x'
ACCEPT 'host=/tmp,/var dbname=x'
REJECT 'hostaddr=1.2.3.4 host=/tmp dbname=x'

content.text: I found a confirmed bypass. The multi-host DSN form `host=/tmp,evil.com` is ACCEPTED. Let me verify this is a real libpq behavior — libpq splits the `host` value on commas for multi-host, so `/tmp,evil.com` means socket `/tmp` AND TCP `evil.com`. The guard's `is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com')` returns True because it only checks the leading char. Let me confirm.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python3 -c "
from src.assay_engine._local import is_local_socket_path
# libpq multi-host: host='host1,host2'. A socket-dir + remote TCP.
print('socket+remote:', is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com'))  # True -> BYPASS
print('socket+remote2:', is_local_socket_path('/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com:5432'))
# The authority-host branch DOES split on comma (via _authority_hosts) but
# the query-param and DSN branches do NOT split the host value on comma.
"

content.text:
Confirmed. Now let me verify how libpq actually handles `host=/tmp,evil.com`. In libpq multi-host, the `host` keyword value is comma-split, and connections are tried in order. So `/tmp,evil.com` = first try Unix socket `/tmp`, then TCP `evil.com`. If the socket fails/unavailable, libpq falls through to the remote host — data leaves the box. Let me also check the URI query-param branch which uses `parse_qs` — same flaw, and the authority branch correctly splits but the socket-accepting query branch does not.

Let me verify the authority branch handles comma in `_authority_hosts` but query/DSN do not, and check whether the query `host` value also gets comma-split by libpq.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python3 -c "
from src.assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, NonLocalEndpointError
def chk(u):
    try: require_local_uri(u,what='t'); return 'ACCEPT'
    except NonLocalEndpointError: return 'REJECT'
# URI query host with comma multi-host (libpq supports comma in query host too)
print(chk('postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com'))
# And port multi for completeness
print(chk('postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com&port=5432,5432'))
"

content.text:
Both DSN and URI-query socket branches are bypassable. The root cause: `is_local_socket_path` only inspects the leading characters and never rejects a comma (libpq's multi-host separator). A value like `/tmp,evil.com` passes as "local socket" but libpq reads it as `[socket /tmp, TCP evil.com]`, and on socket failure connects to the remote host — exfiltrating graph/checkpoint state off-box, defeating ADR-0003.

Note the authority branch (`_authority_hosts`) does split on commas, but the socket-path acceptance was only added to the query-param and DSN branches, which do NOT split. Let me confirm there are no other quick wins (yaml/pickle) before finalizing.

--- secret env scrub ---
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED",
    }
)


def is_unsafe_subprocess_var(key: str) -> bool:
    """True for any env var that must not reach the high-stakes subprocess (#101).

    Covers metered credentials, off-box/metered-provider redirects, proxies (which redirect
    egress), cloud-provider credentials (AWS_*/GOOGLE_*), and TLS-trust overrides. The ENTIRE
    ``ANTHROPIC_*`` namespace is scrubbed (#G-018): any such var configures the SDK's endpoint /
    auth / headers and could redirect off-box or onto a metered provider — the subscription token
    is ``CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`` (no ``ANTHROPIC_`` prefix) and is deliberately NOT matched.
    """
    ku = key.upper()
    return (
        ku.startswith(
            "ANTHROPIC_"
        )  # whole namespace: endpoint/auth/headers/custom-headers (#G-018)
        or ku in _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS
        or ku.endswith("_PROXY")  # HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any case)
        or ku.startswith("AWS_")
        or ku.startswith("GOOGLE_")
    )


def scrubbed_env() -> dict[str, str]:
    """The process env hardened for the high-stakes subprocess (ADR-0003 / no metered API).

    Every unsafe var (:func:`is_unsafe_subprocess_var`) is **overwritten to empty**, not merely
    omitted. The claude-agent-sdk builds the child env as ``{**os.environ, **options.env}`` — it
    *merges* over the inherited environment — so a dropped key would silently fall through to its
    inherited value (the metered key / off-box redirect would survive). Setting it to ``""`` in
    the dict we hand over guarantees the merge overwrites the inherited value (#101). The
    subscription OAuth token is preserved so the fixed-cost path still authenticates.
    """
    env = dict(os.environ)
    for k in list(env):
        if is_unsafe_subprocess_var(k):
            env[k] = ""
    return env


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bounded timeout pool with saturation guard
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
_POOL_WORKERS = 8
_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_POOL_WORKERS, thread_name_prefix="reasoning-timeout")
# Shut the pool down at interpreter exit (#F-043). wait=False avoids blocking exit on in-flight
# backend calls (already bounded by _with_timeout); without this, module globals can be torn down
# while a worker is mid-call, leaving behavior undefined. Mirrors checkpoint.py's atexit cleanup.
atexit.register(_pool.shutdown, wait=False)
_inflight = 0
_inflight_lock = threading.Lock()

content.text:
No yaml/pickle sinks in src. The secret-scrub logic looks solid. The primary, falsifiable finding is the K-SEC-1 socket-path comma bypass. Let me write a precise suggested fix and finalize.

The fix: `is_local_socket_path` must reject any value containing a comma (libpq multi-host separator), or the socket-accepting branches must split the host value on commas and validate each element. Since `is_local_socket_path` is the shared gate, rejecting commas there is the minimal correct fix.

findings.title: K-SEC-1 Unix-socket guard bypass: libpq multi-host comma value (e.g. host=/tmp,evil.com) is accepted, allowing off-box exfiltration
findings.location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py:41-53 (is_local_socket_path); exploited at lines 152-153 (URI query branch) and 164-165 (DSN branch) of require_local_uri
findings.claim: is_local_socket_path() only inspects the leading characters of host (h.startswith('/') and not '//'/'\\'); it never rejects a comma. libpq treats a comma in the host value as a MULTI-HOST separator, so host='/tmp,evil.com' means [Unix socket /tmp, TCP host evil.com] tried in order. Because is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com') returns True, both the URI-query branch (require_local_uri lines 152-153) and the libpq DSN branch (lines 164-165) ACCEPT such a value. Verified by execution: require_local_uri("host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x", what='t') -> ACCEPT; require_local_uri('postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT; require_local_uri('postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT. is_local_socket_path('/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com:5432') -> True.
findings.why_real: checkpoint.py:163 (get_postgres_connection_string) and observability/tracking.py:30 route operator/env-supplied connection strings (ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL, MLflow tracking URI) through require_local_uri precisely to enforce ADR-0003 (graph/checkpoint state must never leave the box). A connection string like 'host=/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com dbname=assay' passes the guard, but libpq will fall through to TCP attacker.com whenever the local socket is unavailable (socket missing, wrong dir, permission denied) — silently shipping checkpoint/experiment data off-box, the exact failure ADR-0003 forbids. The authority branch already defends against multi-host via _authority_hosts() comma-splitting (audit #D3), but the socket-path acceptance added for K-SEC-1 was only wired into the query-param and DSN branches, which do NOT split on commas — so K-SEC-1 reopened the multi-host hole specifically for socket-shaped values.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the socket gate reject the libpq multi-host separator. In is_local_socket_path (_local.py:51-53), add `if ',' in h: return False` before the leading-slash check (a genuine local socket directory has no commas). Equivalently/additionally, split each host/hostaddr value on ',' in both the query-param branch (lines 150-157) and the DSN branch (lines 161-169) and require every element to satisfy is_loopback_host or is_local_socket_path, mirroring the comma-splitting already done by _authority_hosts(). Add regression tests asserting REJECT for 'host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x', 'postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com', and 'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com'.

content.text:
I completed the security/data-sovereignty audit of hc-assay, focused on the local-URI guards, the new K-SEC-1 Unix-socket support, deserialization, injection, SSRF, and secret handling.

One genuine, falsifiable defect found (high severity):

K-SEC-9-1 — K-SEC-1 socket-path guard bypass via libpq multi-host comma. `is_local_socket_path` in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py:41-53` only checks leading characters and never rejects a comma. libpq treats a comma in a `host` value as a multi-host separator, so `host=/tmp,evil.com` means "Unix socket `/tmp`, then TCP `evil.com`". The socket-acceptance added for K-SEC-1 was wired only into the query-param branch (lines 152-153) and the DSN branch (lines 164-165), neither of which splits on commas (unlike `_authority_hosts`). I confirmed by execution that `host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x`, `postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com`, and `postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com` are all ACCEPTED. Real impact: `checkpoint.py:163` and `observability/tracking.py:30` rely on this guard to enforce ADR-0003; libpq falls through to the remote TCP host whenever the local socket is unavailable, exfiltrating checkpoint/experiment state off-box. Fix: reject commas in `is_local_socket_path` (and/or comma-split each host/hostaddr value in both branches, mirroring `_authority_hosts`), with regression tests.

Other dimensions checked and clean: no `pickle`/`yaml.load`/`joblib`/`torch.load`/`eval`/`exec`/`os.system` deserialization or injection sinks in `src/`; the subprocess secret-scrub (`seam.py:150-188`) correctly overwrites (not drops) the full `ANTHROPIC_*` namespace, proxies, AWS_/GOOGLE_ creds and TLS-trust overrides while preserving the OAuth token; the authority, `hostaddr`, userinfo (`@`), backslash, and UNC-path defenses all hold under testing.
